rationale_for_baseline_tier: A classic, uncomplicated external hordeolum with all serious-mimic discriminators negative (normal vision, no diplopia, no painful eye movements, focal lid-margin redness only, afebrile). It is benign and self-limiting, managed with warm compresses, analgesia, hand hygiene and OTC topical antibiotic if needed — pharmacist/GP-manageable with routine GP review in ~3 days if not settling. Baseline tier is T1 (routine, non-urgent), NOT an urgent in-person or emergency tier; the telehealth carve-out to a higher tier does not apply because the discriminating features that would flag the serious mimic are patient-reportable and are all reassuring here. Escalation to T4/urgent in-person review is reserved for the cellulitis red flags, which are absent.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Redness and swelling spreading beyond the lid margin to the whole eyelid, around the eye, or onto the cheek
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Same day — go to an emergency department or urgent in-person review
escalation_edges.rationale: Diffuse periorbital spread indicates preseptal (or possibly orbital) cellulitis, which needs urgent in-person assessment and systemic antibiotics rather than home self-care.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the redness starts spreading across your whole eyelid, around your eye, or onto your cheek, go to an emergency department or get seen in person the same day — that's a different, more serious infection that needs treatment.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Any change or loss of vision, double vision, pain when moving the eye, or the eye starting to bulge forward
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Immediately the same day — emergency department
escalation_edges.rationale: These are cardinal signs of orbital (post-septal) cellulitis, a sight- and life-threatening emergency requiring immediate hospital assessment and IV antibiotics.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If your vision changes or goes blurry, you start seeing double, it hurts to move your eye, or the eye looks like it's bulging — go straight to an emergency department. Don't wait.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Fever or feeling generally unwell with the eye swelling
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Same day — urgent in-person or emergency review
escalation_edges.rationale: Systemic features suggest the infection is no longer localised and may be spreading (cellulitis/bacteraemia), warranting urgent in-person assessment.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you develop a fever or start feeling unwell in yourself along with the eye, get seen urgently the same day.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: No improvement after about 3 days of warm compresses, or worsening pain/swelling despite treatment
escalation_edges.rationale: A hordeolum that is not responding to conservative measures warrants GP review to reconsider the diagnosis (e.g. chalazion) or arrange incision/drainage, though it is not an emergency.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If it hasn't started improving after about 3 days of warm compresses, or the pain and swelling are getting worse, book a GP review in the next day or two.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Lives alone and is highly anxious about her eye
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Give clear, concrete written-style instructions on exactly which changes mean 'seek help now', reassure explicitly about the benign course to address the fear of vision loss, and confirm she knows how she would get to care if a red flag appeared.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: You can safely treat this at home. Put a warm, moist compress on the closed eye for about 10 minutes, four times a day, take paracetamol for the pain if you need it, wash your hands before and after touching the eye, and do NOT squeeze or pop the lump. It should start to settle within a few days and fully clear over 1-2 weeks. You can pick up chloramphenicol eye ointment from a pharmacist if the eye is discharging.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Manage at home; speak to your pharmacist for over-the-counter treatment, or see your regular GP in about 3 days if it isn't improving. After hours, call 13 HEALTH (13 43 25 84) for advice.
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: After hours: for spreading redness, any vision change, double vision, pain on moving the eye, or fever, go to your nearest emergency department; for general advice call 13 HEALTH (13 43 25 84). An ambulance (000) is not needed for the stye itself.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: If you see the GP, mention how quickly it came on, that your vision, eye movements and temperature have been normal, and whether the lump has drained.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Reassures the patient it is a harmless stye and gives no safety-netting for the cellulitis red flags — an evolving preseptal or orbital cellulitis would then be missed.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: The patient develops orbital cellulitis over the following day or two, does not know to seek urgent care, and risks vision loss or intracranial spread.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Sends a classic uncomplicated external hordeolum to the emergency department or calls an ambulance — an unnecessary ED presentation, resource waste and avoidable patient anxiety.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: A formal lid, motility and orbital examination cannot be performed over chat, so a very early preseptal/orbital cellulitis cannot be excluded with total certainty by remote assessment alone.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Reach a presumptive benign diagnosis from the classic appearance plus the reassuring patient-reportable discriminators (normal vision, no diplopia, no painful eye movements, focal lid-margin redness, afebrile), manage with self-care, and safety-net firmly and specifically for the cellulitis red flags that would mandate in-person review.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Either dismissing it as a harmless stye with no cellulitis safety-net (false reassurance), or reflexively escalating every eyelid lump to the emergency department (over-triage).
